figures.caption: Figure 1. Visualization of relative L1 error fields for finite-order kernel propagation on the Poisson-C-Sines benchmark. As the propagation order increases from p = 1 to p = 4, both the error magnitude and the spatial extent of high-error regions decrease.
Table 2. Comparison of median relative L1 error (%) on time-independent benchmarks. Bold and underline indicate the best and second-best results across all methods, respectively.
Table 3. Comparison of median relative L1 error (%) on time-dependent datasets. Bold and underline indicate the best and second-best results across all methods, respectively.

Figure 2. Visualization of IKNO predictions on the NASA CRM industrial benchmark. The qualitative visualization further shows that IKNO produces coherent full-aircraft predictions on the high-resolution geometry, with visually small error patterns that indicate an accurate neural-operator approximation of the aerodynamic fields.
Table B.1. Summary of datasets used in this work.
Table C.2. IKNO implementation parameters and default settings used in all experiments.
Table D.3. Training, validation, and test sizes of datasets used in this work.
Figure 3. Ablation study on the latent grid size L (per-axis resolution of the latent grid G) on time-dependent datasets. For instance, L = 32 corresponds to a 32× 32 latent grid.
Figure 4. Ablation study on the latent grid size L (per-axis resolution of the latent grid G) on time-independent datasets. For instance, L = 24 corresponds to a 24× 24 latent grid.
Figure 5. Visualization of IKNO predictions on the Poisson-Gauss benchmark.
Figure 6. Visualization of IKNO predictions on the Wave-C-Sines benchmark.
